title: Wages rise, rates to follow
full_text:
A higher-than-expected pay boost for workers on the minimum wage will pave the way for another two rate rises this year, with Australia’s lagging productivity and already high inflation to blame, economists and business leaders say.
While Treasurer Jim Chalmers welcomed the Fair Work Commission’s (FWC) ruling yesterday as a “pay rise that millions of Australian workers need and deserve”, industry leaders warned of a “tsunami of insolvencies”, with businesses already under strain from weak consumer sentiment and rising electricity costs and rent.
From July 1, minimum award wages will increase by 4.75 per cent – or from $24.95 to $26.44 – following yesterday’s FWC decision. The above-inflation determination covers about 20 per cent – or 2.8 million – Australian workers, with minimum wages to increase to $1004.90 a week.
AMP chief economist Shane Oliver said with inflation raging at 4.2 per cent, the Reserve Bank would be “worried this will add to cost pressures in the economy, which businesses then pass on as high prices”.
The minimum wage increase will also be used as a benchmark to push for salary rises in other industries.
Dr Oliver yesterday tipped RBA rate rises in August and November, which could push up the cash rate to an 18-year high of 4.85 per cent.
“Of particular concern is that productivity is still low and therefore the ability of businesses to absorb this without pushing up prices is limited,” he told The Daily Telegraph.
While he said the RBA would “head off” a potential wage price spiral by punishing households with rate rises, Dr Oliver said it was a “bad price to pay”. “The better option would have been to see more action in budget to take pressure off inflation via spending cuts,” he said.
Speaking at an event in Melbourne yesterday, RBA board member Ian Harper said inflation was “now expected to be higher than it has been”.
Sydney restaurateur and Kazzi Beach Greek founder Peter Pappas said the FWC’s 4.75 per cent minimum wage increase needed to be viewed alongside previous increases that, when combined, have resulted in a $6.60 boost to the minimum wage since 2020.
Businesses are also forced to pay weekend and public holiday rates, plus superannuation on top of that, he added.
“Our suppliers are telling me that a very high number of their customers are going on to payment plans at a rate that they’ve never seen before,” he said. “I fear that we’re in for a tsunami of insolvencies in hospitality sector.” Business Sydney executive director Paul Nicolaou said while “employees deserve fair wages” and he did not “begrudge” the FWC’s determination, the federal government needed to recognise the “significant impact this increase will have on businesses”.
“The flow-on effects of wage increases extend well beyond payroll costs,” he said.
“Businesses will need to absorb higher operating expenses at a time when consumer spending remains cautious and economic conditions remain challenging.” EDITORIAL PAGE 23
